Page 96
4,5 The Procedure
To eliminate selection 
bias 
the 
companies 
were 
asked to randomly select thirty individuals involved in 
the analysis, design, programming, 
and 
management 
of 
internal information system projects. 
Only one company 
was unwilling to provide a random 
sample 
and 
in 
one 
case 
thirty 
ISDP 
represented 
the entire development 
s t a f f .
Once individuals were selected by 
a 
company 
for 
inclusion 
in 
the 
study 
they were sent a letter from 
either the vice president of information systems or the 
director of personnel requesting their involvement. 
It 
was 
explained 
they 
would 
be 
participating 
in 
an 
attitude 
survey 
conducted 
by 
New 
York 
University 
regarding how they felt about their jobs and companies. 
The 
letter 
also informed the subjects that the survey 
was 
completely 
confidential 
and 
would 
require 
approximately 45 minutes to complete.
To gather the data 
from 
each 
company 
one 
hour 
sessions 
were 
scheduled for on site administration of 
the 
survey. 
Approximately 
ten 
participants 
were 
scheduled 
for 
each 
hour 
session. 
Due to illnesses, 
vacations, and schedule conflicts approximately 
twenty 
five subjects per company completed the survey.
Page 97
Before beginning the survey 
each 
individual 
was 
given 
a 
cover 
letter 
signed by the researcher which 
explained the study, emphasized that 
their 
individual 
responses 
were 
completely confidential, and requested 
they include their name on the front page to allow 
the 
collection 
of 
followup 
data. 
A 
copy 
of the cover 
letter may be 
found 
in 
Appendix 
C. 
Once 
all 
the 
subjects 
had 
arrived 
at 
a 
session 
the 
researcher 
introduced 
himself, 
reiterated 
the 
information 
contained 
in 
the 
cover letter and answered questions 
from the subjects.
The survey was completed in the 
presence 
of 
the 
researcher. 
Four 
questionnaires 
from 
the 
on 
site 
administrations were discarded by the researcher: 
one
for 
excessive 
missing data, one at the request of the 
participant, and two 
because 
the 
subjects 
had 
been 
incorrectly identified as ISDP.
One company would not 
permit 
the 
researcher 
to 
conduct an on-site administration of the questionnaire. 
Rather, company staff 
distributed 
and 
collected 
the 
questionnaires 
at 
training 
sessions. 
These
participants received the 
same 
cover 
letter 
as 
the 
other 
subjects 
but 
were 
requested 
to 
place 
the 
questionnaire in a sealed 
envelope 
addressed 
to 
New 
York 
University. 
The sealed envelope was returned to 
the individual 
in 
charge 
of 
the 
session 
who 
then 
forwarded the survey to NYU. 
A copy of this letter can
Page 98
be found in Appendix D. 
Of 
the 
24 
sealed 
envelopes 
received 
from 
this 
company 
six 
contained 
blank 
questionnaires or questionnaires with excessive missing 
data 
and were discarded from the study. 
The remaining 
eighteen questionnaires were carefully 
scrutinized 
by 
the 
researcher 
to ascertain the validity of the data. 
The questionnaires revealed 
no 
abnormal 
patterns 
of 
responses 
when 
v i s ually 
compared 
to 
the 
other 
r e s p o n s e s .
Summary
This chapter describes the process of building the 
questionnaire, 
selecting subjects for inclusion in the 
study, and the 
administration 
of 
the 
questionnaire. 
The careful manner by which the questionnaire was built 
and administered 
should 
help 
assure 
reliability 
of 
measurement and the valid i t y  of the items. 
The diverse 
industries and random selection of 
individuals 
should 
assure 
the 
g e n e r a l i z a b i 1 ity of the findings and guard 
against 
selection 
bias. 
The 
following 
chapter 
describes the findings of the data collection process.
Page 99
CHAPTER FIVE
5.0 Results and Discussion
This chapter outlines the procedures used to 
test 
the 
model 
and 
hypotheses presented in Chapter Three. 
The results of the 
data 
analysis 
are 
presented 
and 
discussed. 
Path 
analysis 
[Kenny,1979 
;Billings and 
Worten,1978] was used to test 
the 
model 
proposed 
in 
figure 
3.0 while multiple regression and correlational 
analysis [Cohen and Cohen,1975 ] were used to test 
the 
hypotheses 
regarding 
ISDP 
task 
and 
individual 
differences. 
The data were first tested, however, 
for 
reliability. 
Descriptive statistics for each variable 
and correlation coefficients were also generated.

The reliability for each variable 
was 
determined 
by calculating Cronbach's Alpha. 
The reliabilities are 
reported in table 5.0. 
All of 
the 
reliabilities 
are 
above 
the .70 level suggested as acceptable [Nunnally, 
1967].

statistics 
for 
each 
variable 
were 
calculated. 
The 
means,

and maximum values, and 
skew 
for 
each 
variable 
are 
presented 
in 
table 
5.1. 
Finally, 
the 
zero-order 
correlations were calculated among the study variables. 
The correlations are presented in table 5.2.

5.2 Assumptions of Path Analysis
Path analysis is a regression based data 
analytic 
technique 
that 
permits 
the 
testing of causal models 
using cross-sectional data. 
Path analysis assumes 
the 
data 
conform 
to five specific characteristics. 
These 
five assumptions are outlined in 
Billings 
and 
Wroten 
[1978] and are presented below.
1) The relationships 
between 
the 
variables 
are 
linear.
2) The relationships 
among 
the 
variables 
(i.e. 
the regression equation) are additive.
3) The measures have interval scale properties.
4) The model is recursive, no variable feeds 
back 
onto itself.
5) 
The 
model 
is 
fully 
defined, 
that 
is, 
no 
variable 
has 
been 
omitted that could account for the 
observed relationship between two variables.
The data were tested and found to conform 
to 
the 
above 
five 
characteristics. 
The 
procedures used to 
test each assumption follows.
To test assumption one scatterplots were generated 
between 
each pair of variables. 
The scatterplots were 
visually examined and no non-linear relationships 
were 
detected.
Page 105
As 
the 
review 
of 
the 
literature 
revealed 
no 
evidence 
of 
multiplicative or quadratic relationships 
assumption two was assumed to have been met.
As described in chapter 
4, 
this 
study 
employed 
carefully 
constructed measures which used a reasonable 
number of values and contained 
multiple 
items. 
Such 
measures 
yield 
data 
with 
acceptable 
interval scale 
properties [Billings and Wroten, 1978]. 
Assumption 
3 
was therefore met.
Recursiveness requires the model causality 
to 
be 
in one direction only. 
The review of the literature in 
chapter two revealed 
no 
evidence 
of 
feedback 
loops 
which 
would 
violate 
the assumption of recursiveness. 
Based on this literature the model built for testing is 
recursive.
Demonstrating 
that 
assumption 
five 
has 
been 
satisfied 
is difficult. 
There are a host of variables 
that could potentially effect any model. 
Based on 
the 
review 
of 
the 
literature 
however, 
the 
important 
variables have been included. 
Additionally statistical 
evidence 
was generated to determine if important third 
variables were ignored which would 
make 
the 
observed 
relationships in the model spurious. 
Testing for third 
variables was done once the path analysis was concluded 
by 
residualizing 
the variables and then computing the 
correlations among the residuals. 
The results 
of 
the
Page 106
residual 
analysis 
are 
presented 
in 
section 5.4 and 
provide evidence that no important third varaibles were 
omitted.
5.3 Testing the Proposed Model and Building 
a 
Trimmed 
Model
To test the relationships hypothsized by the model 
path 
coefficients 
were 
calculated 
and 
tested 
for 
statistical significance at the .05 (two-tailed) level. 
The coefficients are presented in table 5.2.1.
Of the fourteen hypothesized paths seven were 
not 
significant 
at 
the 
.05 
level. 
Employing 
theory 
trimming [Heise, 1969], these paths were 
deleted 
from 
the 
model 
resulting 
in 
a 
new 
trimmed 
model. 
The 
trimmed model is presented in figure 5.0.
The path coefficients for the trimmed 
model 
were 
computed and are presented in table 5.2.2.
The 
trimmed 
model 
beta 
coefficients 
were 
all 
tested 
for 
significance at the .05 level. 
All of the 
betas were significant 
at 
the 
.05 
level, 
therefore 
further 
theory 
trimming was not performed. 
The final 
trimmed path model with the respective beta weights for 
each path is presented in figure 5.1.

One path not included in the original path 
model, 
which would imply a zero path coefficient, was found to 
be 
significant. 
This 
was 
the 
path 
between 
role 
ambiguity and role conflict. 
The finding of a non-zero 
path between role ambiguity and role conflict 
suggests 
that the path should have been included in the original 
model. 
However, 
this 
study 
does 
not 
provide 
any 
evidence 
regarding 
the causal direction of this path. 
This path was not included in the 
original 
model 
for 
two 
reasons. 
First, 
while 
the 
literature 
review 
revealed that role conflict and role ambiguity tend 
to 
be correlated; 
this was believed to be the result of a 
covariation with a third variable. 
Boundary 
spanning 
was 
expected to account for this covariation. 
Second, 
the model was based on previous research which did 
not 
hypothesize a causal link between these variables. 
For 
the above two reasons a path between role conflict 
and 
ambiguity was not included in the original model.

Reconstructing the Correlation Matrix
Two statistical tests 
which 
can 
be 
applied 
to 
determine 
how 
well 
the 
trimmed 
model 
captures the 
relationships in the sample data 
include 
testing 
for 
omitted 
third 
variables 
and 
reconstructing 
the 
correlation matrix. 
First, the correlations among 
the
Page 112
residuals were computed and are presented in table 5.3.
None of the correlations were significant 
at 
the 
.05 
level. 
This 
provides 
evidence 
satisfying 
assumption five that the trimmed model had not 
omitted 
any important third variables.

Page 114
The final test used to determine the 
adequacy 
of 
the 
trimmed 
model 
was the rebuilding of the orginial 
correlation 
matrix 
from 
the 
trimmed 
path 
model 
[Kerlinger 
and 
Pedazhur, 
1973]. 
To 
reproduce each 
correlation coefficient the direct, indirect and common 
cause effects were calculated and summed.
The direct effects were 
measured 
by 
calculating 
the standardized regression coefficient for each path.
An indirect effect was calculated 
by 
determining 
the 
product 
of 
the 
path coefficients along the path 
from 
the 
causal 
variable, 
through 
the 
intervening 
variables, 
to the effected variable. 
For example, the 
indirect 
effect 
of 
boundary 
spanning 
on 
job
satisfaction 
was 
the product of the path coefficients
from boundary spanning to role conflict 
and 
the 
path 
coefficient 
from 
role conflict to job satisfaction or
.275 times -.391.
A common cause effect is the 
amount 
of 
spurious 
covariation 
between 
two variables. 
For example, role 
conflict and commitment have one 
common 
cause 
effect 
because 
boundary 
spanning effects both commitment and 
role conflict. 
The common cause effect is the 
product 
of 
the 
path 
from 
boundary 
spanning 
to 
commitment 
(.16269) and the path from boundary 
spanning 
to 
role 
conflict (.27582). 
A more detailed description of each 
of these effects and how to calculate them can be found
Page 115
in Stumpf and Hartman [1982],
Table 5.4 presents the three effects, the original 
correlations, 
and 
the 
amount of unexplained residual 
for each reconstructed 
correlation 
coefficient. 
The 
difference 
between 
the 
original 
and 
reconstructed 
correlation (the sum of the direct, indirect and common 
cause 
effects) should be no more than .05 if the model 
effectively captures the relationships present 
in 
the 
data [Billings and Wroten, 1978],

The 
effects 
not 
explained 
by 
the 
model 
are 
generally 
below 
the .05 level, however there are four 
residuals large enough to 
warrant 
further 
attention. 
The 
two 
largest 
deviations 
between the observed and 
recomputed 
correlations 
involve 
role 
conflict 
and 
commitment 
and 
role 
conflict 
and intention to quit. 
These two deviations can be explained by the failure to 
include 
a 
path 
from role conflict to role ambiguity. 
Had a path from role conflict to 
role 
ambiguity 
been 
included 
the 
indirect 
effect 
of 
role 
conflict 
on 
commitment would have been more negative and reduce the 
difference 
between 
the 
observed 
and 
recomputed 
correlations. 
Similarly, 
if 
this 
path 
had 
been 
included 
the large difference between the observed and 
recomputed 
correlation 
between 
role 
conflict 
and 
intention 
to quit would also have been reduced as role 
conflict's indirect effect on intention to 
quit 
would 
have been greater.
The 
differences 
between 
the 
observed 
and 
reconstructed 
correlations 
for 
boundary spanning and 
job satisfaction and boundary spanning and intention to 
quit cannot be explained as a failure to include a path 
between role conflict and role ambiguity. 
rt 
appears 
that 
the trimmed model does not adequately capture the 
effects of boundary spanning on 
job 
satisfaction 
and 
intention 
to 
quit. 
However, 
this 
"failing" of the 
model is quite small, with the 
unexplained 
difference
Page 118
between 
the 
original 
and 
reconstructed correlations 
being just above the .05 cutoff level.
In general, the failure to find large correlations 
between 
the 
residuals and the generally small amounts 
of 
variation 
between 
the 
original 
correlation 
coefficients 
and 
the 
reconstructed 
correlation 
coeffiencts provides evidence that the trimmed model of 
figure 5.1 adequately models the relationships observed 
in the data 
with 
further 
research 
needed 
to 
fully 
understand the relationships between boundary spanning, 
intention to quit and job satisfaction.

This section examines the failings of the 
orginal 
model 
and 
re-examines 
the literature in light of the 
relationships posited by the trimmed model.
One of the major shortcomings of the orginal model 
involved 
the 
hypothesized 
relationships 
between 
boundary spanning, role ambiguity, 
role 
conflict 
and 
job 
satisfaction 
with 
intention 
to 
quit. 
The path 
analysis found no such 
direct 
paths 
and 
accordingly 
they 
were 
removed 
from 
the 
trimmed 
model. 
In the 
trimmed model these 
variables 
impacted 
intention 
to 
quit indirectly operating via commitment.
Commitment and 
intention 
to 
quit 
measure 
very 
similar 
constructs. 
One 
of 
the three factors which 
characterize commitment is a strong desire to 
maintain 
membership 
in 
the 
organization 
and 
a number of the 
commitment scale items measure this desire. 
Therefore, 
when 
commitment 
is 
partialled from the relationships 
between intention to quit and the other study variables 
it 
is 
not 
suprising that no relationships are found. 
The failure 
to 
find 
a 
direct 
relationship 
between 
intention to quit and the other variables may therefore 
be due to the item overlap 
between 
the 
intention 
to 
quit and commitment measures.
The second 
failure 
of 
the 
model 
involved 
the 
relationship 
between 
boundary 
spanning and the other 
model variables. 
No 
direct 
relationship 
was 
found
Page 121
between boundary spanning and job satisfaction contrary 
to expectations. 
This 
finding 
runs 
counter 
to 
the 
prior 
literature 
[Tushman 
and Scanlan, 1981; 
Keller 
and Holland, 1978] 
which 
suggests 
boundary 
spanners 
have 
inherently 
more interesting work and substantial 
informal power 
and 
status 
due 
to 
their 
access 
to 
external 
information 
[Keller and Holland, 1978]. 
The 
failure to find a relationship 
between 
ISDP 
boundary 
spanning 
and job satisfaction may be due to the nature 
of ISDP boundary 
spanning. 
From 
the 
normative 
job
descriptions 
of 
ISDP 
tasks [Murach, 1977] it appears
that the majority of ISDP boundary spanning 
activities 
are 
intra-organizational 
rather 
than
inter-organizational. 
For example, ISDP 
when 
engaged 
in designing systems must cross departmental boundaries 
to gather information from 
the 
relevant 
user 
areas. 
ISDP 
are 
rarely 
required to 
cross 
organizational
boundaries 
for 
the 
development 
of 
a 
new 
system. 
Intra-organizational boundary spanners such as ISDP may 
not, because of 
the 
intra 
organizational 
nature 
of 
their 
job, 
have 
the 
easy access 
to 
external 
job
opportunities 
that 
their 
inter-organizational
counterparts 
havn. 
Additionally, evidence exists that 
intra-organizational 
boundary 
spanners 
have 
closer 
supervision than inter-organizational boundary spanners 
[Miles, 
1980b; 
Miles 
and 
Perrault, 
1976], 
This 
closeness 
of 
supervision 
may 
further 
reduce 
the 
boundary spanner's power, autonomy and 
status. 
These
Page 122
two 
factors may account for the failure to find a link 
between ISDP boundary spanning and job satisfaction.
Boundary spanning 
was 
found 
to 
have 
a 
direct 
positive 
relationship 
with 
role 
conflict 
but 
no 
relationship 
with 
role 
ambiguity, 
contrary 
to 
expectations. 
The 
prior 
empirical evidence [Keller, 
1978; 
Keller 
et 
al, 
1976] 
also 
found 
that 
while 
boundary 
spanning 
was related to role conflict it did 
not 
relate 
to 
role 
ambiguity. 
The 
relationships 
identified 
by 
the 
trimmed 
model 
are 
therefore 
consistent with the prior literature.
A 
rather 
suprising 
finding 
was 
that 
boundary 
spanning 
related 
positively 
to 
commitment 
in 
the 
trimmed 
model 
rather 
than 
negatively 
as 
had 
been 
hypothesized 
by the original model. 
This relationship 
is probably, a result of the individuals, due 
to 
their 
boundary 
spanning activities, being exposed to more of 
the organization. 
This exposure may provide them 
with 
a 
better 
sense 
of 
the 
mission 
and 
purpose of the 
organization and their role within the 
company. 
This 
may 
increase their identification with the company and 
therefore their commitment.
In summary, six of the fourteen paths hypothesized 
by 
the 
original 
model were supported. 
Some evidence 
therefore exists for the causal ordering 
of 
variables 
proposed 
by 
the 
original 
model. 
Employing 
theory
Page 123
trimming [Heise, 1969] a refined or trimmed 
model 
was 
built. 
This refined model was subjected to a post-hoc 
analysis and was found 
consistent 
with 
much 
of 
the 
prior 
empirical 
literature 
and as such probably more 
accurately describes the true relationships 
among 
the 
variables. 
The 
trimmed 
model, 
however, 
reflects a 
hypothesized causal ordering of variables 
which 
while 
consistent 
with 
the 
sample studied must be tested in 
other samples to establish it generalizabi1 ity.
5.6 Hypothesized Task Differences
Between Programmers and Analysts
In this study, very few individuals were 
employed 
solely as either analysts or programmers but rather had 
some 
combination 
of 
both 
duties. 
Therefore, 
each 
individual was assigned a job task score which captured 
the possible combinations of duties. 
The job score was 
computed for each subject by subtracting the average of 
the three programming items from 
the 
average 
of 
the 
five 
analytic 
items. 
This 
produced 
a continuum of 
scores running from a 
possible 
minimum 
of 
-4 
to 
a 
possible 
maximum 
of 
+4. 
An individual who had more 
programming than analytic duties would have a 
negative 
score 
while 
an 
individual 
with more analytic duties 
would have a positive score. 
A 
score 
of 
zero 
would 
indicate 
an 
individual 
whose 
position 
was 
equally
Page 124
weighted 
between 
analytic 
and 
programming 
duties. 
Descriptive 
statistics 
for 
the 
job 
task 
score are 
presented in table 5.6.

The analysis revealed that analytic duties related 
positively 
to 
boundary 
spanning 
and 
role conflict. 
Therefore, systems analysts will span 
more 
boundaries 
and 
experience 
role 
conflict 
than 
their programmer 
counterparts. 
Contrary 
to 
expectations, 
boundary 
spanning 
was 
not 
found 
to relate to role ambiguity. 
These findings are in agreement with the trimmed 
model 
which 
suggested 
that 
boundary spanning would lead to 
role conflict but was unrelated to role ambiguity.
Relationships counter to those 
hypothesized 
were 
found 
for Hdd and Hee. 
Individuals with more analytic 
duties were found to be more committed and less 
likely 
to 
have 
intentions to quit than those ISDP with fewer 
analytic duties. 
These findings 
are 
also 
consistent 
with the trimmed model. 
Individuals with more analytic 
duties also tend to be boundary spanners 
as 
suggested 
by 
Haa. 
The 
model 
would 
predict 
that 
these 
individuals, due to their boundary spanning 
activites, 
would 
have 
enhanced commitment and reduced intentions 
to quit.
The task 
difference 
analysis 
demonstrated 
that 
boundary 
spanning 
is 
an 
elegant and powerful way to 
describe many of the duties of the systems 
analyst 
as 
boundary 
spanning 
and the job task score share 25% of 
their variance. 
Finally, contrary to expectations, 
it 
is 
the 
programmer 
and not the systems analyst who is 
the high risk employee as programmers are 
more 
likely

to 
intentd 
to 
leave 
the 
organization. 
This 
is 
consistent with the finding that systems analysts 
span 
more 
boundaries 
and 
are 
more 
committed 
than 
programmers.

variables

Summary
This chapter described the 
results 
of 
the 
data 
analysis 
with 
regards 
to 
the 
path 
model 
and 
the 
differences hypothesized on the basis of individual and 
task 
characteristics. 
The 
implications 
of 
these 
results for management and future IS research 
will 
be 
discussed in the following chapter.
Page 133
CHAPTER SIX
6.0 Conclusions
This chapter 
presents 
and 
discusses 
the 
major 
findings 
of 
the 
study. 
As this study was unable to 
account 
for 
the 
origins 
of 
ISDP 
role 
ambiguity, 
research 
propositions 
addressing possible sources are 
presented. 
Recommendations 
are 
made 
regarding 
the 
management 
of 
ISDP 
and 
the 
appropriateness of ISDP 
career 
paths. 
Finally, 
recommendations 
for 
future 
study are made.
6.1 Findings of the Study
1) Analysts span more boundaries than programmers. 
The 
major 
hazard 
faced 
by 
analysts 
when they span 
boundaries is role conflict 
which 
negatively 
impacts 
their 
job 
satisfaction, 
commitment, and intention to 
q u i t .
2) Boundary 
spanning 
is 
positively 
related 
to 
commitment, 
which reduces ISDP intention to quit. 
The 
overall effect of boundary spanning is to 
reduce 
ISDP 
intention 
to quit despite its positive relationship to 
role conflict.
Page 134
3) Analysts and programmers are both intolerant of 
role 
ambiguity. 
Of 
the 
variables 
studied 
role 
ambiguity is the 
most 
detrimental, 
having 
a 
strong 
negative 
effect 
on 
job satisfaction, commitment, and 
intention to quit.
4) Programmers are less 
committed 
than 
analysts 
and more likely to express intentions to leave.
5) Role ambiguity 
was 
not 
related 
to 
boundary 
spanning 
nor was it related to analytic or programming 
duties. 
Unfortunately 
this 
study 
does 
not 
provide 
information as to the sources of ambiguity.
6) The individual difference analysis 
found 
that 
only 
age, 
education, and years of DP experience had a 
significant impact on 
ISDP 
intention 
to 
quit. 
The 
relationships, 
however, 
are 
very 
weak 
and 
as such 
provide very few useful guidelines for IS management.
6.2 Potential Sources of ISDP Role Ambiguity
Given the strong detrimental effect 
of 
ambiguity 
on 
ISDP job satisfaction, commitment, and intention to 
quit it is important to identify the potential 
sources 
of 
ISDP 
role 
ambiguity. 
Unfortunately 
good theory 
regarding the antecedents of ISDP role 
ambiguity 
does 
not exist. 
Operationally, role ambiguity refers to the 
extent 
to 
which 
clear 
and 
precise 
information 
is
Page 135
lacking regarding task, reward, and role. 
This section 
will, therefore, examine the structure of ISDP jobs and 
how 
this could potentially impact ISDP role ambiguity. 
Finally, research propositions are developed for future 
test i n g .
One possible source of ISDP role ambiguity is 
the 
structure 
of 
the project teams. 
ISDP may be assigned 
to permanent teams or rotated to different 
teams 
with 
different 
members. 
It 
is expected that ISDP who are 
assigned to permanent 
project 
teams 
will 
experience 
less 
ambiguity as they will be familiar with the other 
team 
members, 
and 
have 
a 
clearer 
idea 
of 
their 
responsibilities 
and 
the 
expectations 
of 
the 
team 
leader thus increasing the clarity of their role. 
This 
may 
not 
be 
true 
where 
team 
m e m bership 
changes 
frequently as these individuals will have less time 
to 
establish precisely what their role is.
It is 
expected 
that 
the 
type 
of 
project 
and 
technology 
may 
also impact ISDP role ambiguity. 
ISDP 
who are working on "state of 
the 
art" 
projects 
that 
involve 
unfamiliar 
technology 
may 
experience 
role
ambiguity as it will be less 
clear 
how 
to 
use 
this 
technology 
than 
familiar 
"routine" 
technology. 
Therefore it is expected that the 
type 
of 
technology 
will effect the clarity of ISDP tasks.
